stem: Which of the following is equivalent to `!(p && q)`?
explanation: De Morgan's first law states !(p && q) equals !p || !q — the negation distributes to each operand and the operator flips from && to ||. Option A incorrectly keeps && instead of flipping it. Options C and D omit the individual negations entirely.

stem: Which of the following is equivalent to `!(p || q)`?
explanation: De Morgan's second law states !(p || q) equals !p && !q — each operand is negated and || flips to &&. Option A incorrectly keeps || instead of flipping it. Option B omits the individual negations. Option D only negates p, leaving q un-negated.

Because y equals 0, the left operand y != 0 evaluates to false. Short-circuit evaluation of && means the right operand x / y > 2 is never evaluated, so no division by zero occurs. The condition is false, so the else branch prints "no". Option C is the intended trap for students who do not know short-circuit evaluation prevents the division.

stem: Which of the following boolean expressions is equivalent to `!(x >= 10)`?
explanation: Negating >= flips it to <, so !(x >= 10) is x < 10. Option A is too restrictive — it excludes x = 10 itself but !(10 >= 10) is false, which is consistent, but x > 10 also excludes x = 9 which should be included. Wait — actually, !(x >= 10) is true when x < 10, confirming D. Option C (x <= 10) is the complement of x > 10, not x >= 10. Option B (x != 10) includes values like x = 11 where the original expression is false.

s1 and s2 are created with new, so they are two distinct objects in memory. == compares references and returns false. The .equals() method compares character content, which is identical for both, so it returns true. Option A would be correct only if s2 = s1 (alias). Option B forgets that .equals() performs content comparison.

stem: Which of the following is equivalent to `!(x != y)`?
explanation: The negation of the != operator is ==, so !(x != y) simplifies directly to x == y. Options A and B impose an ordering that is not implied by equality. Option C is the original un-negated expression.

The left operand n > 5 evaluates to true. Because || short-circuits on the first true operand, the right side n++ > 100 is never evaluated, so the post-increment never executes and n remains 10. Option C is the short-circuit trap: students who assume both sides always evaluate would increment n and print 11.

stem: Which of the following is equivalent to `!(a > 5 || b < 3)`?
explanation: Applying De Morgan's law: !(a > 5 || b < 3) = !(a > 5) && !(b < 3) = (a <= 5) && (b >= 3). Option A flips || to || instead of &&, violating De Morgan's. Option B uses strict inequalities (< and >) rather than the correct <= and >=. Option D negates the operator but not the individual relational operands.

options.label: C
options.text: A compile error occurs because `!` cannot be applied to a compound boolean expression.

explanation: x > 5 is true (8 > 5) and y > 5 is false (3 > 5). true && false = false. Applying ! to false gives true. Option B is wrong because negating false yields true. The ! operator applies to any boolean expression in Java, so Option C is incorrect. Option D describes the un-negated condition x > 5 && y > 5, not the negated one.

a && b = true && false = false, so !(a && b) = !(false) = true. Because the left operand of || is already true, short-circuit evaluation makes the entire condition true without checking c. The if body executes and prints X. Option B would require the entire condition to be false, which it is not.

options.label: C
options.text: The output depends on the runtime values of `a` and `b`.

explanation: a < 5 is true and b > 6 is true, so their AND is true, making result1 = !(true) = false. For result2: a >= 5 is false and b <= 6 is false, so false || false = false. result1 == result2 is false == false, which is true. By De Morgan's law, !(p && q) is always equivalent to !p || !q for all input combinations, confirming these two expressions are logically identical.

stem: Which of the following expressions is equivalent to `!(p || q)`?
explanation: By De Morgan's law, !(p || q) is equivalent to !p && !q — both operands are negated AND the operator flips from || to &&. A keeps the || operator instead of flipping it to &&. C omits the negations entirely. D only negates p.

stem: Which of the following boolean expressions is equivalent to `!(score < 70)`?
explanation: Negating < flips it to >=, so !(score < 70) is equivalent to score >= 70. C uses strict > which would exclude the value 70 itself, and A and B are not negations of the original.

x > 5 is false (4 > 5) and y < 8 is false (10 < 8), so the || expression is false. !(false) is true, so flag is true and "pass" is printed. B results from forgetting to apply the outer ! to the false sub-expression.

options.label: C
options.text: An `ArithmeticException` is thrown because `k / n` divides by zero.

options.label: D
options.text: A compile-time error occurs because `n` is zero.

explanation: Because || short-circuits, Java evaluates n == 0 first; since that is true the entire condition is immediately true and k / n is never evaluated, so no ArithmeticException is thrown. The if-branch executes and prints "yes". C is the classic short-circuit distractor: the division is skipped, not executed.

6 % 2 == 0 is true and 9 % 3 == 0 is true, so (true && true) = true, and !(true) = false, giving x = false. For y: a % 2 != 0 is false and b % 3 != 0 is false, so false || false = false. Both x and y are false — by De Morgan's law the two expressions are logically equivalent. A results from forgetting to apply !, C and D result from computing one variable correctly but not the other.

stem: Which of the following is equivalent to `!(p || q)` for all boolean values of `p` and `q`?
explanation: De Morgan's law states that !(p || q) equals !p && !q — negate each operand and flip || to &&. Option A incorrectly keeps || instead of switching to &&. Options C and D drop the negation entirely.

stem: Which of the following is equivalent to `!(x >= 10 || y == 0)` for all `int` values of `x` and `y`?
explanation: Applying De Morgan's: !(x >= 10 || y == 0) = !(x >= 10) && !(y == 0) = x < 10 && y != 0. Option A uses || instead of &&. Option B incorrectly negates x >= 10 as x <= 10 rather than x < 10. Option D uses x > 10, also an incorrect negation of >=.

Because x is 0, the left operand x != 0 evaluates to false. The && operator short-circuits: the right operand y / x is never evaluated, so no ArithmeticException occurs. The overall condition is false and the else branch prints "small".

c = !true || false = false || false = false. d = !(true && !false) = !(true && true) = !true = false. Both variables are false, so the output is "false false". A common trap is computing d as !(true) && !(!false) = false && false = false — the same result here, but for the wrong reason; the correct path goes through the whole inner conjunction first.

options.label: D
options.text: The entire `return` expression should be wrapped in an outer `!`.

explanation: De Morgan's law gives !(x >= low && x <= high) = !(x >= low) || !(x <= high) = x < low || x > high. The code uses && instead of ||, requiring x to be simultaneously less than low AND greater than high — impossible — so the method always returns false. Replacing && with || fixes the bug.

The condition !(i % 2 == 0 || i % 3 == 0) is equivalent (by De Morgan's) to i % 2 != 0 && i % 3 != 0 — i.e., divisible by neither 2 nor 3. In 1–10 those values are 1, 5, and 7, so count reaches 3. Option A (2) omits 1; option C (4) incorrectly includes a value divisible by 2 or 3; option D (5) counts all odd numbers without applying the mod-3 filter.

stem: Which of the following is equivalent to the boolean expression `!(score >= 70 && hasSubmitted)`?
explanation: By De Morgan's law, the negation of an AND becomes an OR, and `!(score >= 70)` is `score < 70`. Choices B, C, and D either keep the wrong operator, negate the relational test incorrectly, or fail to negate both parts.
`a` is true and `b` is false, so `!(a && b)` is true and `x` becomes 8. The expression `!a || b` is false, so the else branch runs and `y` becomes 2.
